# [Part 2: Social Influence, Groups and Prosocial Behavior]{style="color: #004b23"} {.unnumbered}

This part of the book focuses on how the presence of others or our social relationships can influence our behavior. 

It contains five chapters on classic studies addressing social influence: 

- [Conformity](chapter21.qmd)
- [Obedience to Authority](chapter4.qmd)
- [Social Loafing (Ringelmann Effect)](chapter7.qmd)
- [Social Facilitation](chapter8.qmd)
- [Pretty Privilege: Stereotypes as Self-Fulfilling Prophecies](chapter1.qmd)

Moreover, there are four chapters that are dedicated specifically to social influence exherted by social groups. These chapters cover the following themes: 

- [Minimal Group Effect](chapter9.qmd)
- [Stanford Prison Experiment](chapter10.qmd)
- [Stereotype Threat](chapter11.qmd)
- [Intergroup Contact Theory](chapter12.qmd)

Finally, there are two chapters which address a particular type of social behavior: prosociality. These chapters are dedicated to the following topics: 

- [Bystander Effect](chapter13.qmd)
- [Social Heuristics Hypothesis](chapter14.qmd)
